NETS does not make such as- 944 to the reader of BIBLIOGRAPHICAL NOTE The best commentary on the Letter to date is Carey ther and Jeremiah: The Additions (AB 44; Garden Cit Ier, see Moore’s bibliography, AB 331–332. A copy of a letter that Ieremias sent to those who would be led as captives into Babylon by the king of the Babylonians to proclaim to them just as it was commanded to him by God. On account of the sins that you have sinned against God, you will be led into Babylon as captives by Nabouchodonosor, king of the Babylonians. When, therefore, you come into Babylon, you will be there for rather many years, even for a long time, as long as seven generations. But after this, I will bring you from there with peace.
Now then, you will see in Babylon silver and gold and wooden gods being carried upon shoulders causing fear to the nations.

Beware, therefore, lest you too, having been made like the allophyles, abecome likea them and reverence for themb seizes you 5when you see a crowd before and behind them doing obeisance to them, but say in thought, “It is necessary to bow down to you, O Master.”
For my angelc is with you, and he himself is seeking out your souls.
For their tongue has been smoothed by a craftsman, and they are overlaid with gold and silver. Yet they are fake and are not able to speak.

And just as for a maiden who loves ornamentation, taking gold, 9they construct crowns upon the heads of their gods. But it is also the case that the priests, purloining gold and silver from their gods, will spend it on themselves.
And they will give of them also to the prostitutes at the brothel. And they adorn themb with clothes like human beings—gods of silver and gold and wood.
But they are not kept safe from rust and foodd. When they are dressed in purple clothing, 12they have their face wiped because of the dust from the house, which is rather much upon them.
And hee has a scepter like a human district judge, hee who will not destroy the one who sins against him.
And he has a hand-knife in his right and an ax but will not deliver himself from war and robbers. Whence they are recognizable as not being gods. Do not, therefore, fear them.
For just as when a person’s implement, when broken, becomes useless, 16such are their gods, though they are established in houses. Their eyes are full of dust from the feet of those who enter.

And just as the courtyards are fortified all around against anyone who has wronged the king as though sentenced to death, the priests secure aI.e. are assimilated to bI.e. the gods(’) cOr messenger dP gI.e. is menstruating hOr copper etter of ieremias A. Moore’s Anchor Bible Commentary—Daniel, Es- , NY: Doubleday, 1977). For other literature on Let- BENJAMIN G. WRIGHT their houses with doorways and bolts and bars, lest they be stripped by robbers.
They burn lamps even more than for themselves, none of which theyb can see.

Just like one of the beams of the house they are, and they do not notice that their hearts, it is said, are licked out, as the creeping creatures from the earth devour both them and their clothing, 20blackened in their face by the smoke from the house.
Bats, swallows and birds ride upon their bodies and their heads, just as also the cats do.
Whence you will know that they are not gods. Do not, therefore, fear them.
For the gold, which they wear for beauty, unless someone wipes off the corrosion, they will not make it shine, for they were not sentient, even when they were cast.
Those things in which there is no breath are bought at all cost.

Lacking feet they are carried upon shoulders displaying their dishonor to people, and even those who attend to them are ashamed 26on account of the fact that they are made to stand up by them, lest ite fall to the ground. Not even if some one makes it stand upright, will it move by itself, nor if it leans over, will it straighten itself up, but gifts are placed before them, just as before the dead.
But their priests, by selling their sacrifices, use themb. And likewise also their wives preserve some of themf, but they do not share any with the poor or disabled.
One who gsits apartg and one who has just given birth touch their sacrifices. Since you know, therefore, from these things that they are not gods, do not fear them.
For on what basis can they be called gods? Because women serve up for gods of silver and gold and wood!
And in their houses the priests take their seat with their tunics torn and their heads and beards shaved, whose heads are uncovered.
And they howl and shout before their gods as some do at a funeral feast for a corpse.
Taking from theirb clothing, the priests clothe their wives and children.
Whether they experience evil from someone, or whether it be good, they will not be able to repay. They can neither empower a king nor can they remove him.
Likewise they can neither give wealth nor moneyh. If anyone, professing a vow to them, does not keep it, they will not demand it.
They will not deliver a person from death nor rescue a weak person from a strong one.
They will not restore a blind person to sight; they will not rescue a person who is in anguish. rhaps corrosion eI.e. the god fI.e. the sacrifices lette
They will not have pity on a widow nor trea orphan well.
These things that are made of w and overlaid with gold and silver are like st from a mountain, and those who attend to t will be put to shame.
Why then should the thought to be or be called gods?

Furthermore, though the Chaldeans th selves bring them into disrespect—when they s mute incapable of speech, bring him and req that Bel utter an articulate sound, as though were capable of sensation—41they are incapab abandoning themb, though they themselves observed it. For theyc have no sense.
And women, wrapping cords around themselves, s the streets burning bran as incense!
And w one of them is drawn away by one of those passes by to have sexual intercourse, she chides companion, because she was not valued as she self was, nor has she had her cordd torn.
E thing that happens for themb is fake. How t should one consider or call them gods?
They have been constructed by carpe and goldsmiths; they are nothing other than the artisans want them to be.
And they who struct them will not themselves be long-li
How then indeed are the things made by t going to be gods? For they have left behind lies disgrace for those who come after.
For when or evils come upon themb, the priests take cou with themselves about where to hide with th
How then can one not perceive that they are gods, who save themselves neither from war from calamities?

For being wood and over with gold and silver, it will be known there that they are fake; it will be clear to all nations kings that they are not gods but the work human hands and that there is no divine wor them.
By whom then should it not be kn that they are not gods?
For they will not set up a king of a country, will they give rain to people.
And they will decide their own case or deliver one wh wronged, since they are powerless.

For like c between the sky and the earth they are. For also fire attacks a house of gods who are woode overlaid with gold or silver, their priests flee and come through safely, but theyb will be burned up li aI.e. Bel bI.e. the gods(’) cI.e.the Chaldeans dI.e. f of ieremias 945 an od nes em be me a est hea e of ave the t in en ho her ereryen ters hat oned. em nd war sel mb. not nor aid fter nd of in wn nor not is ows hen or will e the center beams.
And they shall not withstand king or enemies.

How then must it be admitted or reckoned that they are gods? Gods made of wood and overlaid with silver and gold will not be safe either from thieves or from bandits, 57the strong of whom will strip away the gold and silver, and they make off with the clothing theyb wear. Nor shall theyb help themselves.

So it is better to be a king who displays his manliness or a useful vessel in a house, which the owner will use, than these fake gods, or even a door in a house that keeps safe those things that are in it than these fake gods, and a wooden pillar in palaces than these fake gods.
For sun and moon and stars are bright, and when they are sent for a service, they are obedient.
So also lightning, when it flashes, is conspicuous. And the same wind also blows in every country.
And as for clouds, when ordered by God to traverse the entire inhabited world, they accomplish what is ordained.
And the fire sent out from above to destroy utterly mountains and woods will do what is prescribed. But these thingsb are similar neither in appearances nor in powers.
Whence one should neither think them to be nor call them gods, since they are not able either to judge a case or to treat humans well.
Since you know, therefore, that they are not gods, do not fear them.
For they cannot curse or bless kings, 66and they cannot show signs in heaven among the nations or shine like the sun or give light like the moon.
The animals, which, fleeing to shelter, are able to help themselves, are superior to them.
It is clear to us that they are in no manner gods. Therefore do not fear them.
For as a scarecrow in a cucumber field guards nothing, so are their gods of wood and gold and silver overlay.
In the same way also their gods of wood and overlaid with gold and silver are similar to a thorn bush in a garden on which every bird perches, and so also to a corpse thrown out in darkness.
And from the purple and marblee that rots upon them you will know that they are not gods. And finally they will themselves be consumed, and there will be disgrace in the country.

Better, therefore, is a righteous person who does not have idols, for he will be far from reproach. r tying her clothes ePerhaps sparkling cloth Oxford University Press, Inc. publish objective of excellence in resea Oxford Auckland Cape Town Dar e Kuala Lumpur Madrid Mel New Delhi Shang With o Argentina Austria Brazil Chile Guatemala Hungary Italy Jap South Korea Switzerland Thai Published by Oxford
